rescue_reasoning: The verbatim text commits to implementing "recovery of the heat from exhaust air and sewage" using "heat recovery" ventilation or "an EAHP" and adding "EAHP projects" based on payback. These are explicit building heat recovery/efficiency actions that reduce energy use, aligning with the mechanism "heat_recovery_systems → building_heating_energy_reduction." Although the primary gate flagged missing Resource Efficiency tokens, the measure is clearly resource/energy efficiency in buildings. Climate screen is "explicit_self", supporting retention.
policy_statement: Implement heat recovery from exhaust air and sewage in renovated locations (heat recovery ventilation or EAHP) and implement EAHP projects without renovations where economically viable (payback under 15 years).
mechanism_description: The city will implement exhaust-air and sewage heat recovery (including heat recovery ventilation or exhaust air heat pumps) to reduce the energy needed for building heating.
primary_causal_pathway: Same heating service → ↓ energy input (via waste-heat recovery) → ↓ fuel combustion / ↓ electricity use → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Building HVAC and hot water/heating systems are upgraded to include exhaust-air heat recovery/heat recovery ventilation and/or sewage/wastewater heat recovery (often via heat exchangers and heat pumps).  
2) Mediating physical process: Thermal energy that would be expelled to ambient air or lost in wastewater is captured and transferred to incoming ventilation air or a heat distribution loop, raising the effective coefficient of performance and reducing set primary heating input.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower final energy demand for space heating (and sometimes domestic hot water), which reduces upstream energy production and associated GHG emissions (and can also reduce peak heating load).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing delivered heating energy demand by recovering waste heat, thereby lowering GHG emissions from heat generation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism primarily improves efficiency by reusing waste heat to deliver the same heating service with less energy input, which then reduces emissions from energy supply.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the ultimate climate benefit is mitigation (↓ GHG), the defining, first-order mechanism is reduced energy requirement for the same service—i.e., resource/energy efficiency—rather than direct fuel switching or carbon sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: If recovered heat is driven by high-carbon electricity for heat pumps or causes significant refrigerant leakage, net mitigation benefits could be reduced, but the mechanism remains an efficiency improvement. If deployed mainly for cooling/comfort resilience, there could be adaptation co-benefits, but not the dominant effect described.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy implementing exhaust-air or sewage heat recovery for buildings shares the same primary pathway of lowering delivered heating energy via waste-heat capture, so Resource Efficiency is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific mitigation magnitude, electrification interactions, refrigerant management, and whether it is tied to district heating or building-scale systems should be assigned based on policy-level technical details and local grid/heat carbon intensity.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG impact depends on the carbon intensity of electricity (for heat pumps), system design/commissioning, and refrigerant leakage; in rare cases with very low-carbon heat supply already, the dominant benefit may be cost/peak-load reduction more than emissions, but the mechanism remains energy efficiency via heat recovery.
key_indicators: “will be implemented”; “locations being renovated will use … heat recovery or an EAHP”; “EAHP projects will be implemented without renovations”; “economically viable (repayment period of under 15 years)”; “recovery of the heat from exhaust air and sewage”
co_benefits: Improved indoor air quality/ventilation performance; potential comfort improvements and reduced energy bills
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an internal investment decision rule (capex prioritization) rather than a formal mandate/standard; no explicit legal requirement, incentive, or procurement language is stated, so classified as planning-strategy.
